summary: The contract proposal for Sprint 5 (Monsters & AI) is comprehensive, well-structured, and aligned with the sprint plan. Criteria are concrete, testable, and cover all the F5 requirements: distinct AI behaviors, FOV-based aggro, deterministic spawn tables, bidirectional bump-to-attack, monster turn ordering, death handling, rendering, regression coverage, and documentation. Determinism is correctly emphasized. Out-of-scope items appropriately defer features owned by later sprints (Whisperer naming, status effects, A* pathfinding, archetype pools). Regression coverage of sprints 1-4 is listed. Minor observations are offered as non-blocking suggestions below.

coverage.notes: F5 (Monsters & AI) decomposes into: distinct enemy behaviors (C5, C6, C7, C8, C9), spawn tables drawn from a seedable RNG (C11, C12, C20), FOV-based aggro (C10), bump-to-attack extended to monsters (C14), monster turn loop (C13), monster death (C15) and player death (C16), determinism (C17, C20), rendering (C18, C19), tests (C21), docs (C23), and regression of prior sprints (C22). All sprint-plan obligations are addressed.

criteriaQuality.blocking: Each criterion provides clear test steps and a measurable threshold. Numbers (>=5 monsters, >=4 behaviors, >=8 tests, distance decreases by 1, within 2 turns, etc.) make pass/fail decisions unambiguous.
concerns: C7 (ranged): the threshold 'within 2 turns at least one ranged attack landed without the monster ever becoming adjacent' is testable but somewhat lenient; the generator could implement a 'ranged' that briefly steps toward player before firing. Acceptable given the explicit out-of-scope for projectile rendering, but worth noting.
C9 (ambusher): trigger_range is left to the implementer; reviewers must be willing to read the documented value. The test step correctly says 'documented trigger range' so this is acceptable.

C13 (turn order): assumes a 'turn counter' exists; if the implementation does not expose one, the test could fall back to verifying each monster moved exactly once. Suggest the evaluator allow either evidence of single-step advancement.

C18 (rendering): the project is type 'general' (likely text-based roguelike); 'generate a frame' should be interpreted as the textual buffer/snapshot. Should be fine but worth flagging during evaluation.

C21 (>=8 tests): if existing pre-Sprint-5 tests already cover some of these areas, the contract should ideally count NEW tests. As written, '>=8 tests targeting monsters and AI' is acceptable since pre-existing tests are unlikely to target monsters.

regressionCoverage.notes: Sprints 1-4 are all listed. C22 explicitly enumerates regression checks for movement (1/2), FOV/fog-of-war (3), and item pickup (4). Note: harness-state/regression/registry.json currently has an empty 'sprints' map; the evaluator will need to rely on C22's inline integration steps and on rerunning the existing pytest suite, which is exactly what C22 prescribes.

outOfScope.notes: Out-of-scope items correctly mirror downstream sprints (6 save/load, 7 LLM adapter, 8 Whisperer naming, 9 director, 10 chronicle, 11 archetype pools, 12 polish). Status effects, projectile rendering, A*, and boss/unique monsters are explicitly excluded, which protects scope.

suggestions: Consider adding an explicit criterion that monsters block movement (you can't walk through a living monster — bumping into it triggers attack instead). C14 implicitly covers this ('player did not move onto the monster's tile'), so this is optional.
Consider clarifying in C8 the canonical metric for 'distance' (Chebyshev vs Manhattan) to match C6's wording.

C11's 'at least 3 monster types with weights' is weaker than C4's '>=5 monster definitions'; aligning to >=3 in the spawn table while >=5 exist is fine (some may be depth-gated), but the contract could note this asymmetry.
decisionRationale: All criteria are testable with clear thresholds, all F5 sprint-plan obligations are covered, regression scope is explicit, deliverables align with criteria, and out-of-scope correctly defers downstream features. The minor concerns above are quality-of-life suggestions, not blockers. Accept.
